title: New ways to treat atrial fibrillation

Subtle signals in the heartbeat can indicate the onset of atrial fibrillation. Researchers are exploring promising new ways to treat the condition.Credit: Lucidio Studio Inc/Photographer’s Choice RF/GettyThe symptoms of atrial fibrillation can be subtle — a fluttering irregularity in the heartbeat, or fatigue and shortness of breath. And roughly one in four people globally with this common form of arrhythmia have no symptoms at all1.But researchers say it can often portend more serious cardiovascular conditions, such as stroke and heart failure, with a greatly increased risk of death if untreated.“For untreated atrial fibrillation, the prognosis can be quite poor, and the patient’s quality of life often deteriorates,” says Masaharu Masuda, director of the Kansai Rosai Hospital Cardiovascular Center in Hyogo, Japan.Prompt treatment can be critical for patient outcomes. A medical intervention procedure known as catheter ablation can help to restore healthy heart rhythm and avert future complications. Catheter ablation, once generally reserved for atrial-fibrillation patients who did not respond to drugs, is now considered an earlier treatment option for selected patients2.“This reflects not only its efficacy in controlling symptoms, but also improved clinical outcomes overall,” notes Antonio Moretta, a cardiologist and electrophysiologist based in Sarasota, Florida in the United States.The global medical technology company Medtronic is working with cardiologists around the world to explore research questions around established technologies such as thermal ablation and emerging ones, including pulsed-field ablation (PFA).Restoring rhythmMany cases of atrial fibrillation arise from abnormal electrical activity in the myocardial cells that line the pulmonary veins — the four blood vessels that transport newly oxygenated blood from the lungs to the heart.Catheter ablation was developed to treat such defects3. In this procedure, the clinician ‘isolates’ the veins contributing to a patient’s arrhythmia from the heart by using thermal energy, including radiofrequency and cryo, to selectively eliminate cells transmitting abnormal electric signals to the heart.More than 20 years since the initial development of catheter ablation, bioengineers at Medtronic are continuing to work on technological improvements to support clinical practice. For example, 3D mapping technologies help clinicians navigate to the target site in the heart, while the latest catheters incorporate technological developments such as dual energy treatment capabilities that permit both PFA and radiofrequency ablation4.Though the resulting procedure has benefited countless patients, according to Masuda, the method can, have significant limitations in some cases. For example, the radiofrequency energy that inactivates arrhythmia-triggering cells can potentially cause collateral damage to surrounding tissues, including the esophagus and the phrenic nerve, which controls the diaphragm. Heat damage from this treatment can also contribute to pulmonary vein stenosis, a potentially fatal condition in which the pulmonary veins become constricted.“We’ve been reducing the complications in the surrounding organs of the heart as much as possible,” says Masuda. “However, we’re not able to totally eliminate them.”These hazards make it important to select the most suitable energy source for each patient. However, for some patients, the issue goes beyond the choice of energy source. For example, people who have had persistent atrial fibrillation for several years can be very difficult to treat, according to cardiologist Yasuo Okumura, vice hospital director at the Nihon University School of Medicine in Tokyo.“We have to do a lot of procedures” on these patients, says Okumura. “So I wouldn’t recommend catheter ablation in such cases since the burden imposed by the procedures outweigh the benefits.”A shock to the systemPFA is a faster and more specific alternative. Although it has only been around for a few years, PFA may displace conventional methods as the treatment of choice for atrial fibrillation. The first commercial PFA system entered the clinic in 2021, and several companies now manufacture systems for this technique, including Medtronic5.Instead of inactivating cells with heat, PFA applies short electrical pulses that create tiny perforations in the cell membrane, inactivating cells as their contents leak out1. Importantly, this effect is highly selective.“It works primarily on the myocardium,” explains Masuda. PFA was developed with the goal of reducing damage to other tissues and complications.In addition to its safety advantage due to the high selectivity of pulsed field energy for myocardial tissue, PFA offers procedural benefits because of the characteristics of pulsed field energy itself. The application time per pulsed field lesion is shorter than conventional thermal ablation modalities, including standard radiofrequency and cryo ablation. Also, most PFA catheters used in clinics in Japan have broader catheter contact with tissue. This can reduce the number of energy pulses, resulting in less variability in overall procedure time and more predictable procedure scheduling.“We’re able to ablate and isolate the pulmonary veins much faster,” says Moretta. “This reduces the procedure time and the time under anesthesia.”PFA is also easier for doctors to learn than radiofrequency ablation, according to Okumura.In addition to reducing damage to surrounding tissues, “there are certain side effects to conventional ablation that we used to get that we don’t seem to be getting with PFA,” Moretta notes. This includes pericarditis, a painful inflammation of the tissue surrounding the heart. In many cases in the United States, patients can leave the hospital the same day and recover at home.This method is not a panacea. There are regions of the heart where practitioners need to take extra care with PFA to avoid inducing damaging spasms that could trigger a heart attack. There is also a low risk of damaging red blood cells, yielding biochemical byproducts that can lead to downstream kidney damage.But overall, the safety profile appears to be improved relative to older methods, Okumura says. Considering the procedural predictability, ease of adoption, and favorable safety profile, it may become increasingly difficult to justify the continued use of conventional ablation technologies alone when dual-energy systems are more accessible. “I don’t think we will go back to using catheters that only use radiofrequency ablation,” says Okumura.The improved safety profile of this technique means that it potentially could be offered to patients who would otherwise be ineligible, including older and frailer individuals and those with persistent atrial fibrillation, Moretta says.Okumura says there is even the possibility of further improved outcomes in a future where AI algorithms are employed to analyse patient data and help devise tailored interventions that customize the ablation procedure and the medication for each individual.But the greatest gains in the near term would come from making the technique more widely available. “We haven’t really seen the full use of PFA just yet — many institutions still don’t have it,” says Moretta.And outside high-income countries, this treatment is even farther out of reach — something Masuda is keen to change. “I hope we’re able to make the procedure available to more people,” he says.From left: Masaharu Masuda, Antonio Moretta and Yasuo Okumura.
